description_long: This object is a woody seed pod, likely from a Banksia or similar tree, characterized by its elongated, cylindrical shape and rough, textured surface. The pod is covered with numerous prominent, oval-shaped follicles that are arranged in a spiral pattern along its length, each follicle forming a distinct, raised bump. The exterior is dark brown to grayish in color, with a weathered, matte finish and a hard, woody texture. At one end, a short, thick stem is visible, while the other end tapers slightly. The follicles appear to be empty, indicating the seeds have already been released. This type of tree debris is commonly found on the ground in urban parks, sidewalks, or near street trees after falling from the parent tree.
description: A rough, woody seed pod with prominent oval follicles and a dark, weathered surface. It is elongated and covered in raised bumps, typical of tree debris found in urban areas.
description_view_0: Side view showing the full length of the pod, with the spiral arrangement of follicles and the attached stem clearly visible.
description_view_1: End-on view highlighting the rounded tip and the clustered, raised follicles densely packed at the end.
description_view_2: Opposite side view displaying the elongated body and the spiral follicle pattern, with the stem on the right.
description_view_3: View from the stem end, showing the tapering stem and the pod's rough, bumpy surface extending outward.
